title: Chamber (firearms)

infobox.value: of a [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") is the cavity at the back end of a [breechloading](/wiki/Breechloader "Breechloader") weapon's [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") or [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms) "Cylinder (firearms)"), where the [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") is inserted before being fired. The rear opening of the chamber is the **breech**, and is sealed by the [breechblock](/wiki/Breechblock "Breechblock") or the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cavity where a cartridge is inserted before being fired

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/USMC-120801-M-VG714-002.jpg/250px-USMC-120801-M-VG714-002.jpg)](/wiki/File:USMC-120801-M-VG714-002.jpg)

A cartridge being pushed into the chamber of a [Springfield M1903](/wiki/Springfield_M1903 "Springfield M1903")

The **chamber** of a [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") is the cavity at the back end of a [breechloading](/wiki/Breechloader "Breechloader") weapon's [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") or [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms) "Cylinder (firearms)"), where the [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") is inserted before being fired. The rear opening of the chamber is the **breech**, and is sealed by the [breechblock](/wiki/Breechblock "Breechblock") or the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Taurus_627-KLM_357MAG_008.jpg/250px-Taurus_627-KLM_357MAG_008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taurus_627-KLM_357MAG_008.jpg)

Chambers of a [revolver](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver")'s cylinder

The act of *chambering* a cartridge means the insertion of a round into the chamber, either manually or through the [action](/wiki/Firearm_action "Firearm action") of the weapon, e.g., [pump-action](/wiki/Pump-action "Pump-action"), [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action"), [bolt action](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action"), or [autoloading operation](/wiki/Firearm_action#Automatic_actions "Firearm action") generally in anticipation of firing the weapon, without need to "load" the weapon upon decision to use it (reducing the number of *actions* needed to discharge).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_pistol "Semi-automatic pistol") and [single-shot pistols](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol") (such as [Derringers](/wiki/Derringer "Derringer")), [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"), and [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun") generally have a single chamber integral to their barrels, but [revolvers](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver") have multiple chambers in their [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms) "Cylinder (firearms)"), and no chamber in their barrel. Thus, pistols, rifles, and shotguns can usually still be fired with the [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)") removed as long as a [cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") is inserted into the chamber, while a revolver cannot be fired at all with its cylinder [swung out](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms)#Swing-out_cylinder "Cylinder (firearms)") or [broken open](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms)#Top-break_cylinder "Cylinder (firearms)") excepting a [hang fire](/wiki/Hang_fire "Hang fire") or other malfunction.

In firearms design or modification, "chambering" is fitting a weapon's chamber for a particular caliber or round,[[4]](#cite_note-4) so a [Colt Model 1911](/wiki/M1911_pistol "M1911 pistol") is *chambered* for [.45 ACP](/wiki/.45_ACP ".45 ACP") or [.38 Super](/wiki/.38_Super ".38 Super"), or *re-chambered* for [.38/.45 Clerke](/wiki/.38/.45_Clerke ".38/.45 Clerke"). While the majority of firearms are chambered for one caliber, some are chambered for multiple calibers; however firing an oversized or undersized cartridge can be hazardous.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Fluted chamber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Fluted chamber")]

Main article: [Fluting (firearms)](/wiki/Fluting_(firearms) "Fluting (firearms)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Kinematik_Vergleich_zwischen_Patronenlager_mit_und_ohne_Gasentlastungsrillen_bei_einer_kraftschl%C3%BCssig_dynamisch_verriegelten_Feuerwaffe_mit_Flaschenhalsh%C3%BClse_vertikale_Version_CC_BS-SA_4.0.svg/250px-thumbnail.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kinematik_Vergleich_zwischen_Patronenlager_mit_und_ohne_Gasentlastungsrillen_bei_einer_kraftschl%C3%BCssig_dynamisch_verriegelten_Feuerwaffe_mit_Flaschenhalsh%C3%BClse_vertikale_Version_CC_BS-SA_4.0.svg)

Fluted and non-fluted chamber in a blowback firearm

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/DCB_Shooting_G3_Roller_system3.JPG/250px-DCB_Shooting_G3_Roller_system3.JPG)](/wiki/File:DCB_Shooting_G3_Roller_system3.JPG)

Cutaway model of the chamber with gas relief flutes (left) and roller-delayed action of the G3 battle rifle

In the barrel chamber, fluting refers to gas relief flutes/grooves used to ease the extraction of cartridges.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) They may also come in annular and helical forms.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Notable firearms using fluted chambers are the [roller-delayed blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(firearms)#Roller-delayed "Blowback (firearms)") [Heckler & Koch G3](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G3 "Heckler & Koch G3") and [lever-delayed blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(firearms)#Lever-delayed "Blowback (firearms)") [FAMAS](/wiki/FAMAS "FAMAS") and [AA-52](/wiki/AA-52_machine_gun "AA-52 machine gun").

Roller or lever-delayed blowback arms require that the bolt starts moving while the bullet is still in the barrel and the spent case is fully pressurized. Fluting the end of the chamber allows combustion gasses to float the neck and front of the cartridge case providing pressure equalization between the front outer surface of the cartridge case and its interior. The roller-delayed blowback [StG 45(M)](/wiki/StG_45(M) "StG 45(M)") assault rifle prototypes proved pressure equalization fluting is desirable, since the breech of roller or lever-delayed blowback arms is opened whilst under very high internal cartridge case pressure that presses a spent (bloated) cartridge casing against the chamber walls which can cause significant problems during the cartridge extraction phase. Using traditionally cut (non-fluted) chambers in the StG 45(M) resulted in separated cartridge case heads during testing.

### Ported chamber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ported chamber")]

A barrel chamber with pressure relief ports that allows gas to leak around the cartridge during extraction.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Basically, the opposite of a fluted chamber, as it is intended for the cartridge to stick to the chamber wall making a slight delay of extraction. This requires a welded-on sleeve with an annular groove to contain the pressure.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Stepped chamber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stepped chamber")]

A stepped chamber is an expansion ring near the case shoulder. This feature is used to delay the extraction process further, resulting in a very smooth and soft cycling operation. In some other firearms, like the [Seecamp](/wiki/Seecamp "Seecamp") and the [Walther PDP](/wiki/Walther_PDP "Walther PDP"), the stepped chamber is designed to center the projectile as it enters the rifling, which enhances accuracy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Multi chamber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Multi chamber")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Tayakusituho.png/250px-Tayakusituho.png)](/wiki/File:Tayakusituho.png)

Schematic of a multi-chamber gun

Mostly used on artillery guns. Multi chambered is where the barrel has a series of chambers to propel the round down the barrel to increase speed and range. The [Lymann-Haskell](/wiki/James_Richard_Haskell "James Richard Haskell") multi chamber gun is an example.

## Reversed chamber

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reversed chamber")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/.54_Burnside.jpg/250px-.54_Burnside.jpg)](/wiki/File:.54_Burnside.jpg)

Reverse chambered .54 Burnside cartridge

An unusual reverse chamber has been used on firearms such as the [Burnside carbine](/wiki/Burnside_carbine "Burnside carbine"), [Rikhter R-23](/wiki/Rikhter_R-23 "Rikhter R-23") and T168 autocannons.

## Forensics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Forensics")]

The chamber is a key component to the practice of [forensic firearm examination](/wiki/Forensic_firearm_examination "Forensic firearm examination"). The chamber is known to imprint its surface striations irregularities on the [cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") case, in what are called chamber marks, due to the pressure produced when [shooting](/wiki/Shooting "Shooting").[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Such imperfections in chamber may be produced in the manufacturing process or through extensive use.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Such chamber marks are more pronounced on substandard [firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") or when firing from an undersized chamber.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

In recent years there has been a push to automate this process via the use of [automated firearms databases](/wiki/Automated_firearms_identification "Automated firearms identification").[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics") identification has also seen the development of [microstamping](/wiki/Microstamping "Microstamping") technology which purposefully creates chamber marks through engravings on the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") and [breech face](/wiki/Breechface "Breechface").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chamber_(firearms)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]
